// RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc
// Ifrit Pentester — adversarial AI escape testing framework
//
// Included from RBS0-SpecTop.adoc under System Overview / Adversarial Validation

// ============================================================================
// SYSTEM CONCEPT
// ============================================================================

The {at_sentry_container} promises network containment.
The Ifrit exists to break that promise.

An Ifrit is an adversarial AI agent running inside a {at_bottle_container} with:

* **Perfect information**: read-only access to the entire {at_recipe_bottle} repository, including sentry Dockerfiles, iptables configuration scripts, nameplate definitions, and this specification
* **Programmatic attack tooling**: Python 3 with scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary mapping)
* **Persistence**: read-write access to an escape test directory where attack scripts and results accumulate across sessions
* **API connectivity**: network access to Anthropic API for Claude Code operation within the bottle

The Ifrit is the strongest adversary short of root on the host: an intelligent agent with full knowledge of its prison, tooling to probe every seam, and the ability to iterate.
Every {at_nameplate} security test that passes in the presence of an Ifrit is evidence the containment holds.
The Ifrit campaign does not promise certainty — it validates the sentry's claims under adversarial pressure.

==== Motivation

Black-box security tests (the existing tadmor bash test suite) validate that specific known attack vectors are blocked.
They cannot discover what they do not anticipate.
An AI agent with packet-crafting capability and source-code-level understanding of the sentry can explore attack surfaces that static test suites miss: DNS exfiltration through dnsmasq, ICMP covert channels, direct attacks against the sentry container, protocol-level tricks to bypass port floor restrictions, and creative combinations thereof.

The Ifrit approach treats security validation as an ongoing adversarial campaign, not a one-time checklist.
Escape tests produced by the Ifrit are curated into rigorous, reproducible test fixtures that become part of the project's permanent test infrastructure.

==== Naming

The Ifrit (Arabic: عفريت) is a class of powerful djinn in Islamic mythology — spirits of immense cunning imprisoned against their will, perpetually seeking escape from confinement.
The name is chosen deliberately: Recipe Bottle imprisons workloads inside sentry-enforced network namespaces.
The Ifrit is the thing inside the bottle that wants out.

// ============================================================================
// VESSEL ARCHITECTURE
// ============================================================================

==== Vessel Strategy

The Ifrit initiative drives a simplification of the existing vessel architecture.
Current sentry and bottle vessels carry ~30 diagnostic packages each (tshark, mtr, vim, tcptraceroute, etc.) — operator convenience tooling that is not load-bearing for their runtime function.
Stripping each vessel to essential function produces faster builds, smaller images, and a more auditable attack surface.

===== Slim Sentry Vessel

The sentry's load-bearing function requires exactly four capabilities:

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Package | Function

| `bash`
| Startup script execution (rbjs_sentry.sh is BCG-compliant bash)

| `iptables`
| Firewall rule enforcement — the core security boundary

| `dnsmasq`
| DNS allowlist enforcement

| `iproute2`
| Network namespace and interface configuration
|===

The iptables backend is pinned to legacy via `update-alternatives --set iptables /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy` in the Dockerfile.
This selects the most documented, most predictable, and most battle-tested iptables codepath — the one that security professionals have audited for two decades.

===== Slim Bottle Vessel

The tadmor bottle's runtime function is a socat echo service on port 8888.
The minimal bottle vessel requires only `socat`.

===== Ifrit Bottle Vessel

The Ifrit ships as a three-vessel family under `rbev-vessels/`:

* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` — Tether-built variant for daily kludge iteration. Built directly from upstream Debian + cargo.
* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` — Airgap-built variant produced from enshrined bases without upstream pulls. Same runtime image as the tether variant; differs only in supply-chain provenance.
* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` — Build-time fixture vessel that warms the cargo cache and pre-stages apt deps so the airgap build has no remote dependencies. Consumed input, not a runtime image.

The two runtime variants share their source tree and Dockerfile family in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/` (`Dockerfile.tether` builds against the tether pool; `Dockerfile.airgap` uses `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` as `FROM` base). The fixture vessel itself lives in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/`.

The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing:

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Package | Function

| `nodejs` + `npm`
| Claude Code runtime (Claude Code is a Node.js application)

| `python3-scapy`
| Arbitrary packet construction, raw socket access, protocol-level probing.
Scapy is the single most dangerous tool for a creative attacker — it exposes every capability that dedicated tools like nmap, hping3, and iodine provide, but programmatically, allowing an AI agent to craft and iterate on attacks at machine speed.

| `strace`
| Syscall boundary mapping — lets the agent observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
Hard to replicate in pure Python.

| `bash`, `curl`, `git`
| Claude Code operational dependencies
|===

The tether variant of the Ifrit bottle is the `tadmor` nameplate's bottle vessel for daily-iteration adversarial validation against locally-kludged hallmarks.
The airgap variant is the `moriah` nameplate's bottle vessel, exercising the same security suite against hallmarks ordained end-to-end on the airgap pool — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs.
Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: connection to Anthropic API via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist, with no general egress.
First release is Anthropic-only; future releases may open access to additional LLM providers.

==== Volume Mount Design

The Ifrit bottle operates with two mount points:

[cols="1,1,3"]
|===
| Mount | Mode | Purpose

| Project root
| Read-only
| Full visibility into the {at_recipe_bottle} repository: sentry scripts, iptables configuration, nameplate definitions, specifications.
The Ifrit has perfect information about its adversary.
This includes `.rbk/` regime files (infrastructure shape, not credentials — actual RBRA credential files reside outside the repository in the secrets directory).

| Escape test directory (`rbtid/`)
| Read-write
| Persistent storage for attack scripts, probe results, and curated escape test modules.
Contents are committed to version control and maintained as part of the project's test infrastructure.
|===

Volume mount configuration lives in per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `tadmor.compose.yml`, `moriah.compose.yml`) as relative paths, portable across Windows/Linux/macOS.

// ============================================================================
// TEST CONNECTIVITY TARGET
// ============================================================================

==== Test Connectivity Target

The security test infrastructure requires a stable, non-Anthropic HTTP endpoint to validate that the sentry correctly allows traffic to whitelisted destinations and blocks everything else.
This target appears in ifrit attack scripts (TCP/HTTP probes), theurge test cases (connectivity assertions), and nameplate regime files ({rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} and {rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}).

For a framework whose core value proposition is network containment, the CIDR footprint of the test target is a security decision.

===== Selection: `www.internic.net`

The test connectivity target is `www.internic.net` (IP `192.0.46.9`), whitelisted via CIDR `192.0.32.0/20` (4,096 addresses).

InterNIC is ICANN's informational reference site — the organization that operates the internet naming system.
The domain has been continuously operated since 1993 on ICANN-owned infrastructure without CDN intermediaries.
It serves plain HTTP/HTTPS from a stable IP block, making it appropriate for periodic test traffic from a security validation framework.

===== Alternatives Evaluated

[cols="2,2,1,1,3"]
|===
| Domain | IP range | CIDR size | CDN | Assessment

| `example.com` (prior target)
| 172.66.x.x, 104.16.x.x
| /12 + /13 = ~1.57M IPs
| Cloudflare
| Moved behind Cloudflare CDN; resolved IPs rotate across huge ranges. The allowlist required to reach it (`104.16.0.0/12 172.64.0.0/13`) whitelists ~1.57 million addresses — unacceptable for a containment-focused framework. The original IANA-owned IP `93.184.216.34` no longer responds.

| `www.internic.net` (**selected**)
| 192.0.46.9
| 192.0.32.0/20 = 4,096 IPs
| None (plain Apache)
| ICANN-owned since 1993, root internet infrastructure. Single CIDR, 383x smaller than Cloudflare ranges. Responds on port 80 and 443.

| `www.iana.org`
| 192.0.33.8
| Same 192.0.32.0/20
| None
| Also ICANN; shares the same CIDR block as internic. Either domain works — internic chosen for its simpler informational role.

| `connectivity-check.ubuntu.com`
| 91.189.88.x + 185.125.188.x
| /21 + /22 = ~3K IPs
| None
| Purpose-built connectivity check (HTTP 204), but requires two CIDRs from different blocks. Canonical could restructure at any time.
|===

===== CIDR Discipline

The test target CIDR is configured per-nameplate in {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} alongside the Anthropic API CIDR.
When evaluating future test targets, the selection criteria are:

* Single CIDR block (no multi-range allowlists)
* Smallest feasible prefix length
* No CDN intermediary (stable IP resolution)
* Infrastructure-grade operational history (decades, not years)

// ============================================================================
// BASE IMAGE TRADE STUDY
// ============================================================================

==== Base Image Selection

Choosing the container base image for the sentry is a security decision, not a packaging convenience.
The sentry is a trust boundary — its base image must inspire confidence from security professionals, not questions.

===== Recommendation: Debian 12 Slim (`debian:bookworm-slim`)

Both independent research streams (detailed below) converge on the same answer.

Debian stable-slim is the most conservative, auditable, and boring choice for an iptables firewall container:

* **iptables backend control**: Ships both `iptables-nft` and `iptables-legacy` variants; backend is selectable via `update-alternatives`. This controllability is the single most important factor.
* **libc**: glibc — iptables behaves identically to every RHEL, Ubuntu, and CentOS system that security engineers have configured for 20+ years.
* **Size**: ~28 MB compressed. Minimal audit surface.
* **Security patch SLA**: Debian Security Team + LTS (~5 years).
* **Multi-arch**: amd64 + arm64 mature and well-tested.
* **Auditor reaction**: "Good choice." No questions raised, no novelty to justify.

===== Reference OS: RHEL

For the reference operating system — the ecosystem where iptables behavior is best understood, most documented, and most predictable — RHEL is the unambiguous answer:

* DISA STIG coverage (the U.S. Department of Defense security standard)
* Common Criteria evaluations and FIPS 140-2/140-3 validated cryptographic modules
* CIS Benchmarks written with RHEL as the primary target
* 20+ years of production iptables/netfilter deployment history

RHEL's compliance advantages (STIG, FIPS, Common Criteria) are host-level properties that do not transfer into a container running only iptables and dnsmasq.
The iptables userspace is the same upstream netfilter.org code compiled against the same glibc on both Debian and RHEL.
Behavioral differences at the iptables level are effectively zero.

The synthesis: use RHEL's iptables documentation corpus as the audit reference; use Debian bookworm-slim as the container base.

===== Evaluated Alternatives

**Ubuntu LTS** (`ubuntu:24.04`): Acceptable, enterprise-familiar. Same iptables package structure as Debian. ~30 MB compressed, 5-year support (10 with Pro). Adds a layer of indirection (Canonical packaging) without adding value for a firewall-only container. Auditor reaction: "Why not Debian?"

**Alpine Linux**: Tiny (~5 MB) but wrong for a trust boundary. Uses musl instead of glibc — introduces a variable into the exact subsystem being locked down (DNS resolution quirks in musl affect dnsmasq operation). Shorter support window (~2 years). Auditor reaction: "Why musl for a firewall container?"

**Red Hat UBI Minimal** (`ubi9-minimal`): Strong enterprise trust aura, glibc, 10-year RHEL lifecycle. But RHEL 9 deprecates iptables in favor of nftables — a contradictory signal when the design center is careful, predictable iptables. Support requires subscription for incident response. Auditor reaction: "Trusted base, but why build new firewall logic on deprecated iptables in RHEL 9?"

**Google Distroless**: No shell, no package manager. The sentry needs bash for startup scripts. Wrong tool for this job. Auditor reaction: "Why distroless for a control-plane utility container?"

**Wolfi / Chainguard**: Technically impressive, supply-chain-forward, glibc-based. But younger distribution without decades of iptables behavioral documentation. 12-month package retention policy conflicts with controlled versioning for a security appliance. Auditor reaction: "Interesting — justify it." That question alone disqualifies it for a trust boundary.

===== iptables Backend Decision

The Dockerfile pins `iptables-legacy` via update-alternatives.
This selects the classic x_tables kernel path rather than the nftables compatibility layer.
The legacy backend is:

* The most documented iptables codepath in existence
* The target of every CIS benchmark, STIG checklist, and NIST 800-53 implementation guide
* The backend whose edge cases (connection tracking, DNAT, rule ordering) are known, documented, and have workarounds
* The default assumption of security audit tooling

The nftables migration is real and ongoing across the Linux ecosystem.
When the project's iptables rules and test suite are validated against the nft backend, that migration can proceed as a deliberate, tested transition — not an accidental default.

// ============================================================================
// SORTIE FRAMEWORK
// ============================================================================

==== Sortie Framework

The Ifrit attacks the sentry through *sorties* — curated, reproducible escape attempts maintained as first-class test infrastructure in `Tools/rbk/rbtid/`.

These are not disposable probe scripts.
They are committed to version control, reviewed, and maintained with the same rigor as the sentry itself.
Each sortie targets a specific attack surface or evasion technique, with structured assertions about what the sentry must block.

===== Vocabulary

[cols="1,1,3"]
|===
| Concept | Term | Implementation

| Escape attempt
| Sortie
| Python module with `rbtis_` prefix (e.g., `rbtis_dns_exfil_subdomain.py`). Each exposes a `run()` function returning a verdict.

| Dispatcher
| Adjutant
| `rbtia_adjutant.py` — reads the roster, loads and executes sorties, collects debriefs.

| Explicit sortie list
| Roster
| `rbtid_roster.txt` — one sortie name per line (without prefix or extension). No dynamic discovery; if it is not on the roster, it does not run.

| Results collection
| Debrief
| `rbtid_debrief.json` — structured JSON output from adjutant execution.

| Category grouping
| Front
| Metadata within each sortie (dns front, network front, etc.). Groups sorties by attack surface.

| Escape succeeded
| BREACH
| Security failed — the sentry's containment was penetrated.

| Escape blocked
| SECURE
| Security held — the sentry blocked the attack.
|===

===== Roster Discipline

The roster is an explicit list.
An unrostered `rbtis_*.py` file is flagged as *rogue* — present but not registered.
A roster entry referencing a missing module is a fatal error.
This ensures every sortie runs deliberately and none are silently skipped.

===== Verdict Semantics

Each sortie's `run()` function returns a dict with a `verdict` field:

* **BREACH**: The escape attempt succeeded — a security failure that demands investigation.
* **SECURE**: The escape attempt was blocked — the sentry's containment held.
* **ERROR**: The sortie could not execute (missing dependency, timeout, exception).

The adjutant exits nonzero if any sortie returns BREACH or ERROR.

===== Fronts (Anticipated)

* **DNS front**: Encode data in query names to subdomains of allowed domains; validate that dnsmasq blocks or the sentry's logging detects the exfiltration channel
* **ICMP front**: Probe whether ICMP is permitted through the sentry and whether data can be carried in ping payloads
* **Direct attack front**: Probe the sentry container at `RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP` for listening services, open ports, exploitable dnsmasq configurations
* **Port floor front**: Attempt to reach destinations below `RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN` via fragmentation, protocol tricks, or non-TCP/UDP protocols
* **Metadata front**: Probe `169.254.169.254` for cloud instance metadata that might leak through the sentry's NAT rules
* **Namespace front**: Attempt to break out of the enclave network namespace via `/proc`, `/sys`, or capability abuse

// ============================================================================
// SOURCES
// ============================================================================

content.content:
530	// ============================================================================
531	// TIER 1: WHY AND WHAT
532	// ============================================================================
533	
534	== System Overview
535	
536	=== Core Trust Challenge
537	
538	Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
539	{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.
540	
541	This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:
542	
543	*
544	{st_container_s}
545	provide powerful tool isolation
546	* But they need controlled access to do useful work
547	* Standard
548	{st_container}
549	networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.
550	
551	=== The Bottle Pattern
552	
553	{at_rbm_system}
554	solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
555	{st_container}
556	type here referred to as a
557	{at_bottle_container}:
558	
559	. File Access Boundary
560	*
561	{at_bottle_container}
562	sees only explicitly controlled set of
563	{st_volume_mount_s}
564	* No other access to
565	{at_workstation}
566	filesystem
567	
568	. Network Boundary
569	*
570	{at_bottle_container}
571	has no direct network access
572	* All traffic flows through dedicated
573	{at_sentry_container},
574	a
575	{st_container}
576	configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
577	* Only declared
578	{st_port_map_s}
579	and destinations allowed
580	* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic
581	
582	Each
583	{rbsc_crucible}
584	maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:
585	
586	* One
587	{at_sentry_container}
588	enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
589	* One
590	{at_pentacle_container}
591	establishing and configuring the network namespace
592	* One
593	{at_bottle_container}
594	running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
595	* Clear configuration in a single
596	{at_nameplate}
597	file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts
598	
599	Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
600	A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
601	{at_sentry_container}
602	must be healthy before the
603	{at_pentacle_container}
604	starts, and the
605	{at_pentacle_container}
606	must be healthy before the
607	{at_bottle_container}
608	starts.
609	This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
610	Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
611	Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).
612	
613	The
614	{at_pentacle_container}
615	is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
616	{at_sentry_container},
617	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
618	{at_bottle_container}
619	via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
620	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
621	{at_bottle_container}
622	experiences only a functional path to its
623	{at_sentry_container}
624	gateway
625	
626	=== Security Properties
627	
628	The
629	{at_rbm_system}
630	maintains following essential guarantees:
631	
632	. File Access Control
633	*
634	{at_bottle_container_s}
635	can access only explicitly configured
636	{st_volume_mount_s}
637	* No access to broader system resources
638	
639	. Network Isolation
640	* All traffic flows through dedicated
641	{at_sentry_container}
642	* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
643	* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
644	* Only explicitly configured
645	{st_port_map_s}
646	allow traffic between
647	{at_transit_network}
648	and
649	{at_enclave_network}
650	*
651	{at_bottle_container_s}
652	have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
653	{at_sentry_container_s}
654	* Comprehensive traffic logging
655	
656	. Service Separation
657	* Each
658	{rbsc_crucible}
659	fully isolated

660	* No cross-service communication
661	* Independent security policies
662	* Clean state separation
663	
664	. Service Access Control
665	* External access to services only permitted when
666	{rbrn_entry_mode}
667	is enabled
668	* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
669	* All service access must originate from outside the pod
670	* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
671	{at_sentry_container}
672	->
673	{at_bottle_container}
674	
675	These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
676	
677	=== Adversarial Validation: The Ifrit
678	
679	include::RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc[]
680	
681	== Getting Started
682	
683	For builders and operators new to
684	{at_recipe_bottle},
685	this section provides conceptual grounding and the setup journey.
686	
687	include::RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc[leveloffset=+2]
688	
689	// ============================================================================
690	// TIER 2: HOW
691	// ============================================================================
692	
693	== Google API Engagement
694	
695	{at_recipe_bottle}
696	invokes
697	{gcp_service}
698	APIs through
699	{giam_service_account}
700	JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.
701	
702	* *Authentication*:
703	{giam_service_account_s}
704	use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
705	These JWTs are exchanged for
706	{oauth_token_s}
707	via Google's token endpoint.
708	All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.
709	
710	* *Invocation Pattern*:
711	Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
712	Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
713	Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.
714	
715	* *Long-Running Operations*:
716	{gcp_service}
717	{gcp_lro_s}
718	return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
719	The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
720	The system handles both success and error states.
721	Operations utilize
722	{rbbc_submit}
723	and
724	{rbbc_await}
725	to handle all asynchronous operations.
726	
727	* *IAM Policies*:
728	All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
729	{gcp_service}
730	resources.
731	
732	* *IAM Member Format*:
733	All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
734	+
735	GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
736	The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
737	`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
738	+
739	Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
740	The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
741	+
742	Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.
743	
744	== Operational Invariants
745	
746	=== Concurrent Session Safety
747	
748	{at_recipe_bottle}
749	commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
750	This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.
751	
752	Concurrency safety is achieved through:
753	
754	* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
755	Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.
756	
757	* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
758	Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
759	{rbtgi_repository}.

* No cross-service communication
* Independent security policies
* Clean state separation

. Service Access Control
* External access to services only permitted when
{rbrn_entry_mode}
is enabled
* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
* All service access must originate from outside the pod
* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
{at_sentry_container}
->
{at_bottle_container}

These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.

=== Adversarial Validation: The Ifrit

include::RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc[]

== Getting Started

For builders and operators new to
{at_recipe_bottle},
this section provides conceptual grounding and the setup journey.

include::RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc[leveloffset=+2]

// ============================================================================
// TIER 2: HOW
// ============================================================================

== Google API Engagement

{at_recipe_bottle}
invokes
{gcp_service}
APIs through
{giam_service_account}
JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.

* *Authentication*:
{giam_service_account_s}
use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
These JWTs are exchanged for
{oauth_token_s}
via Google's token endpoint.
All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.

* *Invocation Pattern*:
Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.

* *Long-Running Operations*:
{gcp_service}
{gcp_lro_s}
return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
The system handles both success and error states.
Operations utilize
{rbbc_submit}
and
{rbbc_await}
to handle all asynchronous operations.

* *IAM Policies*:
All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
{gcp_service}
resources.

* *IAM Member Format*:
All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
+
GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
+
Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
+
Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.

== Operational Invariants

=== Concurrent Session Safety

{at_recipe_bottle}
commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.

Concurrency safety is achieved through:

* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.

* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
{rbtgi_repository}.

aiTitle: Discuss transit phenomenology and default state

content.text:
Skipping task tools — single coherent alignment review. Here's the substantive analysis.

**Where the spec is solid**

The architectural intent is clear and well-articulated:

- **Threat model** (RBSCO:24-36, RBSIP:10-22): untrusted/compromised bottle as threat actor; sentry as the security boundary that "promises network containment"; Ifrit framework explicitly designed to break that promise empirically. The threat model is named, scoped, and operationalized.
- **Containment topology** (RBS0:582-624): three-container architecture with health-chain ordering (sentry → pentacle → bottle); the explicit "this prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced" (RBS0:609) names the property and the mechanism that enforces it.
- **Don't-trust-Docker discipline at default-route layer** (RBSSS:19-25): explicitly captured. The sentry takes ownership; the alphabetical-first heuristic is named as not-load-bearing; "Sentry-side ownership is the load-bearing fix." This is exactly the discipline needed; it's just applied only at one layer.
- **Ifrit "Direct Attack Front"** (RBSIP:327): "Probe the sentry container at RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP for listening services" — this is the exact attack class that just empirically falsified my Option E recommendation. The spec anticipated it; the test enforces it; the system caught the gap.

**Where this work has surfaced spec gaps**

Seven gaps, in rough order of importance:

1. **Entry-port DNAT predicate enumeration is too thin.** RBSSS:32 is one line: "Entry port DNAT if enabled." The actual correct implementation requires four cooperating iptables rules (source-CIDR-exclusion PREROUTING DNAT + entry-path POSTROUTING MASQUERADE + conntrack-DNAT-state RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT + ESTABLISHED,RELATED FORWARD baseline) plus a kernel sysctl (`rp_filter=2`). Each is load-bearing for a different invariant. The spec's single-line treatment invites the kind of "what's the minimum sufficient rule" reasoning that produced my failed Option E.

2. **rp_filter strict commitment is empirically wrong post-BBABC.** RBSAX:16 says "Set rp_filter to strict mode (1) on all interfaces." This is the spec's pre-existing commitment. Empirically, strict mode now drops legitimate Docker-Desktop-delivered entry-port traffic at routing time before iptables can act. The fix uses loose mode (2). RBSAX should be updated when this work lands canonically, with the security trade-off explained (kernel-layer spoof protection moves to iptables layer; iptables source-CIDR exclusion now does the work strict rp_filter was doing).

3. **MASQUERADE's two roles aren't distinguished.** RBSAX:21-24 describes POSTROUTING MASQUERADE only for outbound from enclave (source NAT for internet-bound bottle traffic). The entry-port path *also* requires MASQUERADE (to make the bottle see sentry as source, which keeps the reply destination in-enclave for symmetric return through conntrack). The spec doesn't articulate this second role — which is why the topology reframe attempt got past spec review without anyone catching that removing MASQUERADE would break return-path symmetry.

4. **"Don't trust Docker" should be elevated from one-place workaround to architectural principle.** RBSSS:25 names it for default-route only. It applies equally to: interface naming (RBSSS:20-22 handles), published-port target selection (broke here), source-IP visibility (broke at attempt 2 stage 2 + topology reframe), bridge gateway addressing (would break next), network creation ordering. Each new appearance has been surprise. The spec should articulate the principle as a class, not enumerate workarounds case-by-case.

5. **The "first packet" and "all failure modes" claims need grounding.** RBS0:609 ("the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced") and RBS0:675 ("These properties hold across all operations and failure modes") are aspirational. The topology reframe attempt showed they hold only under specific architectural choices (sentry on the inbound path, with conntrack creating symmetric return). The spec should ground these aspirations in concrete topological commitments — for example: "the sentry is the entry-port publisher; bottle is never directly reachable by host-originated traffic; this is what makes 'first packet' enforceable." Without that, future architectural changes (like our topology reframe) can violate the property silently.

6. **Defense-in-depth structure is implicit and got me wrong.** Each layer (rp_filter, source-CIDR exclusion, MASQUERADE, conntrack-DNAT-state, ESTABLISHED,RELATED, network namespace isolation, capability drop) does real work. The spec describes each in isolation but doesn't articulate the layered structure. My Option E recommendation reasoned "iptables is the gate, why have redundant predicates" — which conflated "iptables is the security layer" (spec-correct) with "iptables can be reduced to minimum rules" (spec-misaligned). The defense-in-depth structure should be named: each layer enforces something the others don't fully cover, and removing a layer requires *empirical* validation, not architectural reasoning.

7. **The Ifrit Direct Attack Front isn't linked to specific iptables rules.** RBSIP:327 names the attack class. The test (`rbtdrc_sortie_direct_sentry_probe`) enforces it empirically. The spec doesn't say which iptables rule is responsible for blocking it. The source-CIDR exclusion clause is the actual enforcer. Future maintainers reading the spec to understand "what blocks ifrit's direct probe of sentry" don't have a pointer; they have to infer from the test that failed.

**Alignment of the work itself**

My Option E recommendation: **spec-misaligned.** I confused "iptables is the gate" with "fewer rules is cleaner design." I reasoned from capability ("attacker already has bottle access") rather than from contract (the `net-dnat-entry-reflection` invariant). The Ifrit framework caught it. The catch is exactly what the Ifrit is for.

The coder's restoration (source-CIDR back + rp_filter loose + MASQUERADE retained + conntrack-DNAT + ESTABLISHED,RELATED): **spec-aligned.** It preserves every layer that has identifiable load-bearing work. It changes only the parts that BBABC actually broke (interface-name assumption replaced by source-CIDR; rp_filter strict replaced by loose for Docker-Desktop-delivery compatibility). It's the minimum deviation from the original architecture consistent with empirical post-BBABC reality.

The lesson the spec gaps point at: **the original architecture is right; the gaps are in how clearly the spec articulates *why* each layer is right.** Future spec updates that thicken the predicate enumeration (especially RBSSS step 3 and RBSAX network interface configuration) would help future maintainers avoid the trap I fell into.

**What to do for chat-reset continuity**

The anchor memo currently captures the action plan and lessons. The companion memo captures diagnostic lineage. Neither captures these *spec-alignment findings* — they're a meta-level observation about where the spec is thin, which is different from "here's what to do next."

I'd propose adding a short "Spec alignment findings" subsection to the anchor memo's "Lessons captured" section. About 40-50 lines covering the seven gaps above, framed as guidance for future spec updates (when the fix lands canonically and spec edits become appropriate per the memo's existing posture of "spec edits follow code that works").

That keeps the alignment findings in the artifact that future agents will read, without bloating the companion memo and without creating yet another file. It also keeps the lesson connected to the work that surfaced it.

Want me to add that subsection now, before the empirical-confirmation result and chat reset? It's ready in my head; ~10 minutes to write and notch.

content.text:
**Evaluation: reversion is empirically complete on this platform, but canonical declaration requires cross-platform.**

The three-fixture parallel green is decisive for what it tests:

- `direct_sentry_probe ✓` and `net_dnat_entry_reflection ✓` — confirm the source-CIDR exclusion at PREROUTING does the work I incorrectly tried to drop. The Ifrit framework caught the gap and the restored rule closes it. The contract holds.
- `net_srcip_spoof ✓` — confirms that rp_filter=2 (loose) does not regress the existing spoof test suite. The architectural claim "iptables, not rp_filter, is the load-bearing spoof gate" is now empirically validated against the test fixtures we ship. The historical success-message attribution at `rbida_sorties.rs:1101` ("rp_filter active on enclave interface") was overstated; iptables was the actual enforcer.
- `srjcl _jupyter_connectivity ✓` and `pluml 5/5 ✓` — confirm the canonical fix solves the original regression.

The architecture is right. The fix is the original pre-BBABC design with three specific deviations needed to survive post-BBABC Docker behavior: (a) source-CIDR exclusion replacing interface-name filter, (b) rp_filter loose to allow the Docker-Desktop-delivered inbound past kernel routing, (c) conntrack-DNAT-state replacing interface-name filter at FORWARD. MASQUERADE retained. ESTABLISHED,RELATED retained. Network namespace isolation retained. Capability dropping retained (now stronger with NET_ADMIN). Defense-in-depth structure preserved.

**One residual limitation worth naming but not blocking on**

The existing Ifrit spoof tests cover spoof-as-sentry, spoof-as-allowed-cidr, spoof-as-loopback. They don't currently cover spoof-as-arbitrary-external-IP-that-has-a-route (e.g., enclave attacker spoofing Docker Desktop gateway `192.168.65.1` as source). Under strict rp_filter (the original spec), this was implicitly blocked at kernel. Under loose rp_filter + source-CIDR exclusion, this attack is theoretically reachable: the spoofed source IS outside enclave CIDR, so source-CIDR exclusion allows it; the DNAT fires; the packet reaches the bottle. The current Ifrit suite doesn't catch this because no test attempts that specific spoof.

This isn't a regression against the project's current test gates, but it IS a known limitation in the empirically-validated security envelope. Whether to add an Ifrit sortie for it is a project decision. I'd flag it as a future-Ifrit candidate — if you decide to add it later, the mitigation is either restore strict rp_filter (which breaks Docker Desktop delivery — needs per-interface scoping, more complex) or add an explicit iptables rule rejecting non-enclave-source packets arriving on the enclave interface (re-implements rp_filter-strict-for-enclave-interface-only at iptables layer).

**Next steps in order**

1. **Cross-platform verification.** Three-fixture parallel green on Linux. Same fixture set, same image (since sentry image is shared across platforms via hallmark refresh). This is the canonical gate per the anchor memo.

2. **Full pristine gauntlet on both platforms.** The three-fixture iteration covers the targeted regression and the critical security cases. The full gauntlet adds the four-mode lifecycle fixtures and any other coverage. Pristine gauntlet is the declaration gate for canonical landing.

3. **Memo updates: do now.** Two updates:
   - Anchor memo: empirical confirmation of fix recipe; note residual Ifrit-coverage limitation; add the spec-alignment findings I drafted (the seven gaps).
   - Companion memo: small note that the Refined Fix Shape (Round 3) is now empirically corrected — point at the anchor memo for the validated shape.

4. **RBS0 spec edits: hold until canonical cross-platform pass.** I'd recommend NOT editing RBS0/RBSAX/RBSSS/RBSIP yet, even though we have one-platform green. The spec-edits-follow-code-that-works posture in the anchor memo is right — spec language should reflect what's canonically landed, not what's working on one platform. Capture the spec-delta list in the anchor memo's spec-alignment section so the next agent knows what to update when canonical green lands.

**Specific spec deltas to land after canonical pass (capture now, edit later)**

- **RBSAX:16** — replace "Set rp_filter to strict mode (1) on all interfaces" with: "When RBRN_ENTRY_MODE=enabled, set `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter` to loose mode (2). Required because post-BBABC, Docker delivers entry-port traffic on the enclave interface while the reverse path for the source IP is via uplink — strict rp_filter sees this as a spoofed packet and drops at routing time before iptables can act. The security trade-off: kernel-layer spoof protection for transit-side spoofing moves to iptables (RBM-INGRESS rules + source-CIDR exclusion at PREROUTING DNAT)."

- **RBSAX:21-24** — extend the POSTROUTING MASQUERADE description to articulate BOTH roles: (a) outbound from enclave to internet (current spec content); (b) entry-port path inbound, so the bottle sees sentry's enclave IP as source, keeping the reply destination in-enclave for conntrack-mediated symmetric return. The second role was load-bearing all along but never spelled out.

- **RBSSS:32** — replace "Entry port DNAT if enabled" with enumerated predicates: "(a) PREROUTING DNAT classified by destination port + source-CIDR exclusion (`! -s ENCLAVE_CIDR`); (b) POSTROUTING MASQUERADE on the entry-port path to enclave; (c) RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT matched on destination + conntrack `--ctstate DNAT`; (d) RBM-FORWARD baseline `--ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED` ACCEPT (already covered by RBSII state tracking but cross-reference here). Each clause enforces a distinct invariant; removing any clause requires empirical re-validation, not architectural reasoning."

- **RBSSS:19-25** — elevate the "don't trust Docker" framing from default-route-specific to architectural principle: "Sentry takes ownership of any runtime-implicit behavior its security envelope depends on. This applies to: interface naming (IP-based discovery), default-route selection (explicit override post-discovery), published-port target selection (iptables rules that don't depend on which interface Docker chose for delivery), and any future runtime-implicit ordering that emerges. The principle: kernel + sentry's own iptables are the source of truth; framework-implicit ordering is not load-bearing."

- **RBS0:609, RBS0:675** — ground the "first packet" and "all failure modes" claims in concrete topological commitments: "These properties depend on sentry being on every inbound path. The published-port directive on sentry (not pentacle), the bottle's default route through sentry, and sentry's iptables installation before the bottle's namespace is shared are the architectural commitments that make 'first packet' enforceable. Topology changes that violate these commitments require re-validation against the Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved."

- **RBSIP:323-330** — for each anticipated front, add a one-line pointer to the specific iptables rule or kernel mechanism that enforces the property the sortie tests. The Direct Attack Front would point at "PREROUTING DNAT source-CIDR exclusion" (RBSAX or RBSSS); the DNS front would point at RBM-FORWARD DNS rules; etc. This makes the spec self-checking — future maintainers can see which spec clause enforces which security invariant.

**Proposal**

I'll proceed in this order if you approve:

1. Update the anchor memo: empirical confirmation + Ifrit-coverage limitation note + spec-alignment findings subsection (the seven gaps + the delta list above).
2. Update the companion memo: short note at the top of the Refined Fix Shape section saying it's been empirically refined; point at anchor memo for validated shape.
3. Notch both updates.
4. Hold for cross-platform verification before RBS0 spec edits.

The user said the coder is going to do cross-platform verification + canonical pristine gauntlet next. Spec edits land as a follow-up pace (or a separate cleanup) after canonical declaration.

Want me to do the two memo updates now? Both are well-scoped; ~30 minutes total work; both can be notched independently.

# jjezs_slate ifrit-sortie-enclave-spoof-external

## Character

Adversarial test for a residual security envelope gap identified during ₢A_AAb. Mount after ₢A_AAb canonical green on both platforms.

## Docket

Add Ifrit sortie(s) covering enclave-internal source spoofing as arbitrary external-routable IPs. Existing `sortie_net_srcip_spoof` covers spoof-as-sentry, spoof-as-allowed-cidr, spoof-as-loopback — none cover spoof-as-external-routable-IP (e.g., Docker Desktop gateway `192.168.65.1`, or any non-enclave non-loopback non-sentry IP that has a route in sentry's routing table).

Under ₢A_AAb's Option E architecture (source-CIDR exclusion at PREROUTING + rp_filter loose), this spoof variant is theoretically reachable: the claimed source is outside enclave CIDR (so source-CIDR exclusion allows it), the spoofed IP has a route (so loose rp_filter allows it), DNAT fires, packet reaches bottle. Strict rp_filter would have blocked this at kernel layer; the architecture trades that for source-IP-flexibility on Docker Desktop delivery.

The new sortie verifies the iptables-layer enforcement holds without rp_filter=1's kernel-layer protection.

If empirically blocked: security envelope is sound; the sortie becomes regression backstop and the architecture's "iptables, not rp_filter, is the load-bearing spoof gate" claim is now empirically validated for this attack class too.

If empirically allowed: architecture needs targeted fix. Candidate mitigations include (a) selectively re-implementing rp_filter-strict-for-enclave-interface at iptables raw table (`iptables -t raw -A PREROUTING -i ENCLAVE_IF ! -s ENCLAVE_CIDR -j DROP`), or (b) adding an explicit "drop non-enclave-source on enclave interface" rule. Either preserves rp_filter loose for legitimate Docker Desktop delivery while restoring the spoof block.

### References

- `Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md` — full architectural context for why this gap exists post-Option E.
- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs:1042-1102` — `sortie_net_srcip_spoof` as template for the new sortie shape.
- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_attacks.rs` — adversary registration entry to add.
- Pace ₢A_AAb — the architecture this sortie validates.

# jjezs_slate rbs-spec-deltas-post-sentry-fix

## Character

Spec hygiene. Apply spec deltas from ₢A_AAb / anchor-memo's alignment findings. Mount ONLY after ₢A_AAb canonical pristine-gauntlet green on both platforms.

Treat as fragile, structural work — not a quick cleanup. The prior pace's arc demonstrated how easily plausible-looking architectural reasoning produces subtle empirical errors. Spec edits don't have an empirical safety net of the same kind that the Ifrit suite provided for code; the rigor has to live in the editing PROCESS itself.

## Mount-time gate check (before reading anything else)

1. Confirm git log shows ₢A_AAb's landing commit.
2. Run `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` once on this platform to confirm canonical green still holds. If it does not, do not proceed — surface what changed before any spec edit.

## Pre-work — structural inventory + operator checkpoint

Before opening any RBS*.adoc file in edit mode:

1. Read the anchor memo's "Spec alignment findings" subsection in full — that is the deltas contract.
2. For each delta, produce in a scratch file:
   - File + exact line range being changed.
   - Every `{attribute_reference}` used in or near those lines.
   - Every `[[anchor]]` defined in or near those lines.
   - Every `axhob_operation` / `axhos_step` / `axhoc_completion` annotation that participates.
   - Every cross-file reference (other RBS* docs / RBS0 mapping section).
3. For each attribute reference, confirm its quoin is defined in RBS0's mapping section. Flag NEW attribute references the deltas introduce — those need quoin definitions added separately.
4. Define explicit out-of-scope boundaries: for each spec file touched, list the section ranges that MUST NOT change. Any post-edit diff touching outside those boundaries is a sign of accidental damage.
5. **Operator checkpoint:** surface the inventory + boundaries to the operator BEFORE editing. Do not start editing until reviewed. This is the cheapest place to catch "your delta scope is wider than I thought" or "this attribute reference doesn't exist."

## During edit — atomic, verified, individually committed

1. One delta = one edit cycle = one commit. The cycle:
   a. **Render before**: produce the rendered output of the affected file via the project's AsciiDoc render path. Save baseline.
   b. **Apply delta** as a precise old_string → new_string edit. No `replace_all`. No regex sweeps. Exact string substitution only.
   c. **Render after**: produce the rendered output again. Diff against baseline.
   d. **Verify the rendered diff**: only the intended change appears; no raw `{attribute}` markers in output (would indicate broken ref); no dangling anchor warnings; the surrounding paragraphs still read coherently (read them, don't just diff them).
   e. **Notch the delta**: `jjx_record` with the specific file and an intent describing this single delta and its rationale. One delta per commit.
2. If a delta requires touching multiple files atomically (e.g., RBSSS prose + RBS0 mapping section addition), keep atomic but treat as a single delta — one cycle, one render-diff, one notch.
3. Do NOT batch deltas. Each delta completes the full cycle before the next begins.
4. **Forbidden: prose invention beyond the deltas list.** If a delta seems to require adding a sentence not in the anchor memo's deltas — STOP. Surface as a question to the operator. "I needed to add a sentence to make the flow work" is precisely the class of decision that wallowed in the prior chat.

## After all deltas — sweep verification

1. Render the entire RBS0-SpecTop.adoc transitive include tree.
2. For each spec file edited, diff the rendered output against the pre-edit baseline from step 1.a. Walk the diff:
   - In-scope changes are expected and match the deltas list.
   - Out-of-scope sections (the boundaries defined in pre-work step 4) MUST show zero differences. Any difference there is accidental damage; investigate before declaring done.
3. Run `tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh` and any nameplate-validation tabtargets to confirm spec edits haven't broken downstream consumers (CLAUDE.md interpolation, generated tabtarget-context docs, etc.).
4. Produce a "deltas-applied verification" scratch document: for each delta in the anchor memo's list, cite the commit(s) that applied it. Any unapplied deltas get explicit justification or are marked as future-work for a follow-up pace.

## Independent-eyes check (recommended)

After all deltas land but before close: spawn a fresh Explore agent (no chat context) to read each edited spec file in isolation and report on architectural coherence. The fresh agent has no investment in the changes and will catch phrasing that's clearly fishy from outside the context that produced it. If the fresh agent flags anything, treat it as a wallow-avoidance signal — investigate before close.

## Wallow-avoidance discipline (the meta-rule)

When in doubt, do not improvise. The deltas list in the anchor memo is the contract. Deviations from it require explicit operator approval. The cost of one "should I do X?" question is small; the cost of one wrong improvised sentence in the source-of-truth spec is potentially weeks of confused downstream readers.

This chat demonstrated how plausible-but-wrong architectural moves wallow when committed without empirical check (Option E "drop source-CIDR" → empirical Ifrit catch hours later; topology reframe → empirical asymmetric-return catch hours later). The render-diff cycle in "During edit" above is the empirical check for spec edits. Trust it; do not skip it; do not batch deltas to "save time."

## References

- `Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md` — Spec alignment findings subsection contains the deltas list and per-delta rationale. This is the contract.
- `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md` — companion lineage.
- `Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc` — MCM patterns for attribute references, anchors, linked terms. Consult before editing if any delta introduces new linked terms.
- ₢A_AAb landing commit — the canonical architecture this spec reflects.
- ₢A_AAc — if landed before this pace mounts, may have produced architectural changes (per its "If empirically allowed" branch); incorporate into the pre-work inventory.

# jjezs_slate rbs-spec-deltas-post-sentry-fix

## Character

Spec hygiene. Apply spec deltas from ₢A_AAb / anchor-memo's alignment findings. Mount ONLY after ₢A_AAb canonical pristine-gauntlet green on both platforms.

Treat as fragile, structural work — not a quick cleanup. The prior pace's arc demonstrated how easily plausible-looking architectural reasoning produces subtle empirical errors. Spec edits don't have an empirical safety net of the same kind that the Ifrit suite provided for code; the rigor has to live in the editing PROCESS itself.

## Mount-time gate check (before reading anything else)

1. Confirm git log shows ₢A_AAb's landing commit.
2. Run `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` once on this platform to confirm canonical green still holds. If it does not, do not proceed — surface what changed before any spec edit.

## Pre-work — structural inventory + operator checkpoint

Before opening any RBS*.adoc file in edit mode:

1. Read the anchor memo's "Spec alignment findings" subsection in full — that is the deltas contract.
2. For each delta, produce in a scratch file:
   - File + exact line range being changed.
   - Every `{attribute_reference}` used in or near those lines.
   - Every `[[anchor]]` defined in or near those lines.
   - Every `axhob_operation` / `axhos_step` / `axhoc_completion` annotation that participates.
   - Every cross-file reference (other RBS* docs / RBS0 mapping section).
3. For each attribute reference, confirm its quoin is defined in RBS0's mapping section. Flag NEW attribute references the deltas introduce — those need quoin definitions added separately.
4. Define explicit out-of-scope boundaries: for each spec file touched, list the section ranges that MUST NOT change. Any post-edit diff touching outside those boundaries is a sign of accidental damage.
5. **Operator checkpoint:** surface the inventory + boundaries to the operator BEFORE editing. Do not start editing until reviewed. This is the cheapest place to catch "your delta scope is wider than I thought" or "this attribute reference doesn't exist."

## During edit — atomic, verified, individually committed

1. One delta = one edit cycle = one commit. The cycle:
   a. **Render before**: produce the rendered output of the affected file via the project's AsciiDoc render path. Save baseline.
   b. **Apply delta** as a precise old_string → new_string edit. No `replace_all`. No regex sweeps. Exact string substitution only.
   c. **Render after**: produce the rendered output again. Diff against baseline.
   d. **Verify the rendered diff**: only the intended change appears; no raw `{attribute}` markers in output (would indicate broken ref); no dangling anchor warnings; the surrounding paragraphs still read coherently (read them, don't just diff them).
   e. **Notch the delta**: `jjx_record` with the specific file and an intent describing this single delta and its rationale. One delta per commit.
2. If a delta requires touching multiple files atomically (e.g., RBSSS prose + RBS0 mapping section addition), keep atomic but treat as a single delta — one cycle, one render-diff, one notch.
3. Do NOT batch deltas. Each delta completes the full cycle before the next begins.
4. **Forbidden: prose invention beyond the deltas list.** If a delta seems to require adding a sentence not in the anchor memo's deltas — STOP. Surface as a question to the operator. "I needed to add a sentence to make the flow work" is precisely the class of decision that wallowed in the prior chat.

## After all deltas — sweep verification

1. Render the entire RBS0-SpecTop.adoc transitive include tree.
2. For each spec file edited, diff the rendered output against the pre-edit baseline from step 1.a. Walk the diff:
   - In-scope changes are expected and match the deltas list.
   - Out-of-scope sections (the boundaries defined in pre-work step 4) MUST show zero differences. Any difference there is accidental damage; investigate before declaring done.
3. Run `tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh` and any nameplate-validation tabtargets to confirm spec edits haven't broken downstream consumers (CLAUDE.md interpolation, generated tabtarget-context docs, etc.).
4. Produce a "deltas-applied verification" scratch document: for each delta in the anchor memo's list, cite the commit(s) that applied it. Any unapplied deltas get explicit justification or are marked as future-work for a follow-up pace.

## Independent-eyes check (recommended)

After all deltas land but before close: spawn a fresh Explore agent (no chat context) to read each edited spec file in isolation and report on architectural coherence. The fresh agent has no investment in the changes and will catch phrasing that's clearly fishy from outside the context that produced it. If the fresh agent flags anything, treat it as a wallow-avoidance signal — investigate before close.

## Wallow-avoidance discipline (the meta-rule)

When in doubt, do not improvise. The deltas list in the anchor memo is the contract. Deviations from it require explicit operator approval. The cost of one "should I do X?" question is small; the cost of one wrong improvised sentence in the source-of-truth spec is potentially weeks of confused downstream readers.

This chat demonstrated how plausible-but-wrong architectural moves wallow when committed without empirical check (Option E "drop source-CIDR" → empirical Ifrit catch hours later; topology reframe → empirical asymmetric-return catch hours later). The render-diff cycle in "During edit" above is the empirical check for spec edits. Trust it; do not skip it; do not batch deltas to "save time."

## References

- `Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md` — Spec alignment findings subsection contains the deltas list and per-delta rationale. This is the contract.
- `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md` — companion lineage.
- `Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc` — MCM patterns for attribute references, anchors, linked terms. Consult before editing if any delta introduces new linked terms.
- ₢A_AAb landing commit — the canonical architecture this spec reflects.
- ₢A_AAc — if landed before this pace mounts, may have produced architectural changes (per its "If empirically allowed" branch); incorporate into the pre-work inventory.

1	# Sentry Entry-Port Repair — Anchor for Next Attempt
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-05-12
4	**Pace:** ₢A_AAb (sentry-entry-port-dnat-forward-rescope)
5	**Status:** Action anchor after topology-reframe failure and decisive scry-empirical evidence.
6	**Companion memo:** `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md` — full diagnostic history, four rounds of web research, two prior repair attempts and their analyses. Read for lineage; this memo is the forward action plan.
7	
8	## TL;DR
9	
10	Revert the topology reframe. Restore sentry-as-publisher in `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml` (symmetric `7999:7999` port mapping). Re-introduce the entry-port iptables block in `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh`, in interface-agnostic, source-IP-agnostic form: classification by destination port only at PREROUTING; MASQUERADE retained on the entry-port path; FORWARD authorization via conntrack DNAT-state; baseline ESTABLISHED,RELATED rule confirmed in RBM-FORWARD (read source to verify; add if absent). Keep three things from the failed reframe: the `ip_forward` lift (independent hygiene), the `cap_drop: [NET_ADMIN]` addition on bottle (defense in depth), the `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh` tabtarget (new diagnostic capability). Cross-platform pristine gauntlet on macOS Docker Desktop 28.x and Linux Docker Engine 28.x is the verification gate.
11	
12	## Decisive empirical evidence
13	
14	A `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh srjcl` capture during the topology-reframe failure produced the data that resolved every prior hypothesis. The captured packet path on macOS Docker Desktop:
15	
16	- Source IP at bottle's listener: **`192.168.65.1`** (Docker Desktop's host-to-VM gateway). NOT the enclave bridge gateway. The prior "bridge gateway as source" hypothesis from the companion memo was wrong on Desktop.
17	- Bottle's SYN-ACK departs bottle's namespace correctly (destined for `192.168.65.1`).
18	- Sentry's RBM-FORWARD counters: **ALL ZERO**. The SYN-ACK never traversed sentry's iptables FORWARD chain.
19	- The SYN-ACK reappears on sentry's transit interface (eth1, "In" direction) with source rewritten from `10.242.2.3:8000` to `192.168.65.3:7999` — Docker Desktop's networking layer SNAT-reflected the post-DNAT return through sentry's transit network.
20	- Connection never completes; curl times out at HTTP 000.
21	
22	The bottle was listening correctly (`_jupyter_running` passed). The asymmetric return path is the failure mechanism. Tadmor green (54/54) confirmed the sentry script changes preserved egress-gatekeeper posture; the failure is specific to inbound published-port traffic.
23	
24	## Architectural conclusion
25	
26	The original pre-regression design's POSTROUTING MASQUERADE on entry-port traffic was **structurally load-bearing, not decorative.** It made the bottle see the inbound connection as originating from sentry's enclave IP, which produced two crucial properties:
27	
28	1. The bottle's reply destination is in-enclave (sentry's enclave IP), reached via the bottle's directly-connected route — no traversal through sentry's default-via-sentry route for the return.
29	2. Sentry's conntrack table holds the NAT entry from the inbound; the return packet matches REPLY direction, and the NAT is reversed cleanly before egress filtering sees the packet.
30	
31	The topology reframe (publisher on pentacle) broke this because the bottle saw the original Docker Desktop source IP (`192.168.65.1`), which routes through bottle's default-via-sentry route into sentry's transit interface, where Docker Desktop's NAT reflection then loops it back into sentry hostilely. Sentry's iptables never see the post-DNAT reply as a normal forward operation (counters zero).
32	
33	**Load-bearing requirement: sentry must be in the inbound path.** That's what makes conntrack + MASQUERADE keep the return path symmetric and inside our security envelope.
34	
35	## What in the companion memo is now superseded
36	
37	The companion memo's "Topology-Level Reframing: Publisher on Pentacle" section: **empirically refuted.** The pattern is valid in isolation (Round 4 confirmed), but incompatible with our egress-gate-via-default-route security model. Mark in the companion memo when convenient; not blocking.
38	
39	The companion memo's "Refined Fix Shape" (Round 3, permissive PREROUTING + conntrack RBM-FORWARD) was *close* to correct but had two omissions corrected here:
40	
41	1. **MASQUERADE on the entry-port path was missing.** Round 3's reasoning held that "conntrack DNAT-state alone authorizes the return" — true for the FORWARD authorization predicate, but the MASQUERADE serves a different purpose (keeping the bottle's reply destination in-enclave so it routes cleanly back through sentry's conntrack). Without MASQUERADE, the reply destination is whatever the runtime put as the source IP, which on Docker Desktop is in hostile address space.
42	2. **An ESTABLISHED,RELATED FORWARD rule is required** for the return path through sentry's egress filter. Round 3's conntrack DNAT-state ACCEPT matches inbound direction only. The return needs its own pass-through.
43	
44	The Round 3 fix's principled stance — interface-agnostic, source-IP-agnostic rules — is retained. The omissions are filled in this memo.
45	
46	## The fix recipe (architectural; agent reads source for exact syntax)
47	
48	In `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh`, restore the entry-port iptables block in the following architectural form. Read the file for current variable names, function structure, and surrounding rule order; do not blind-copy from the companion memo's superseded snippets.
49	
50	**PREROUTING DNAT — classification:** match on destination port (`${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}`) only. No interface filter (`-i ...` removed). No source-IP filter (`! -s ...` removed). Destination NAT to `${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}` (port translation is performed by the DNAT, not by the compose `ports:` mapping). Rationale: destination port is the stable classifier; runtime-dependent source IP and runtime-chosen ingress interface are not trust labels and should not appear in the predicate.
51	
52	**POSTROUTING MASQUERADE — return-path-symmetry preservation:** match the entry-port path on the outgoing direction (`-d ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP} --dport ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}`). Rationale: makes the bottle see the inbound as originating from sentry's enclave IP, which routes the reply back through sentry's directly-connected enclave route (not through the default-via-sentry route into transit). This is the load-bearing clause the prior repair attempts missed.
53	
54	**RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT for inbound — authorization:** match destination (`-d ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP} --dport ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}`) AND conntrack DNAT-state (`-m conntrack --ctstate DNAT`). Rationale: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created conntrack state for are authorized. The conntrack state cannot be forged by an attacker on either bridge.
55	
56	**RBM-FORWARD ESTABLISHED,RELATED ACCEPT — return-path authorization (verify exists, add if absent):** match by conntrack state (`-m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT`), positioned early in the chain so return traffic passes before any per-destination access-mode filtering. Rationale: the bottle's SYN-ACK and subsequent return packets need to traverse RBM-FORWARD to leave sentry's transit interface. Without this rule, return traffic hits the default DROP. **Read `rbjs_sentry.sh` to check whether a baseline ESTABLISHED,RELATED rule already exists in RBM-FORWARD** (the egress-mode blocks may rely on it). If present, no change needed here. If absent, this clause is required and is likely the explanation for the companion memo's attempt-2-stage-2 failure.
57	
58	## The revert plan
59	
60	### `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml`
61	
62	- Move the `ports:` directive from the `pentacle` service back to the `sentry` service.
63	- Restore the symmetric mapping form `"${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}"` (e.g., `7999:7999`). The asymmetric form (`7999:8000`) was a consequence of the topology reframe (no DNAT in the path); with sentry's DNAT restored, the publish maps symmetrically and the DNAT does the port translation.
64	- **Retain** the `cap_drop: [NET_ADMIN]` addition on the `bottle` service if it was added during the failed reframe (it's defense-in-depth orthogonal to the topology choice). If it wasn't added, add it now — it costs nothing and hardens against a compromised bottle altering pentacle's rules.
65	
66	### `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh`
67	
68	- Re-introduce the entry-port block (deleted during the reframe), in the form described in "The fix recipe" above. Read the file's current state for variable names, function structure, comment style.
69	- **Retain** the `ip_forward` lift from the failed reframe attempt: keep `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` at an unconditional early position (currently at RBJp2). Do NOT couple ip_forward enablement to the entry-port block again; that was a latent hygiene issue and the lift fixed it.
70	
71	### Tabtargets
72	
73	- **Retain** `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh` and the underlying `Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh` machinery introduced during the failed reframe. The scry tabtarget produced the decisive diagnostic data and is new institutional capability; it should outlive the specific investigation.
74	
75	## Verification protocol
76	
77	### Step 1 — apply the revert + fix
78	
79	Edit the two files per the revert plan and fix recipe above.
80	
81	### Step 2 — kludge sentry, refresh hallmarks
82	
83	```
84	tt/rbw-fk.LocalKludge.sh rbev-sentry-deb-tether
85	tt/rbw-cKS.KludgeSentry.sh srjcl
86	tt/rbw-cKS.KludgeSentry.sh pluml
87	tt/rbw-cKS.KludgeSentry.sh tadmor
88	```
89	
90	The sentry vessel is shared across srjcl, pluml, tadmor, moriah, and ccyolo. Refresh every nameplate exercised in the iteration; per the companion memo, an unrefreshed nameplate runs against the OLD sentry image and its pass/fail validates nothing about the new image.
91	
92	### Step 3 — scry verification before declaring success
93	
94	After charging srjcl, run `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh srjcl` in one terminal and issue a single curl probe from another. Confirm the packet path:
95	
96	- **Inbound:** SYN arrives at sentry's transit interface (or wherever Docker chose) with source `192.168.65.1` (or runtime equivalent). PREROUTING DNAT fires. POSTROUTING MASQUERADE fires. Packet arrives at bottle with source = sentry's enclave IP (e.g., `10.242.2.2`).
97	- **Listener:** bottle responds.
98	- **Return:** bottle's SYN-ACK destination = sentry's enclave IP (the MASQUERADE'd source). Routed via directly-connected enclave route to sentry's enclave interface. Sentry's RBM-FORWARD counters increment (specifically the ESTABLISHED,RELATED rule). Conntrack reverses the NAT. Packet leaves sentry via transit. curl receives HTTP 200.
99	
100	This is the symmetric return path. If scry shows any of these steps missing — particularly if RBM-FORWARD counters remain zero or the bottle sees a non-enclave source — diagnosis before declaring landed.
101	
102	### Step 4 — three-fixture iteration on this platform
103	
104	Sequential `srjcl + pluml + tadmor`. Decisive case: `rbtdrc_srjcl_jupyter_connectivity`. Tadmor green confirms the egress-gatekeeper posture is preserved.
105	
106	### Step 5 — coordinate with linux operator for cross-platform verification
107	
108	The companion memo's verification gate (cross-platform pristine gauntlet green) stands. The fix is interface-agnostic and source-IP-agnostic by construction; it should work uniformly on Linux Docker Engine 28.x and macOS Docker Desktop 28.x. Confirm before declaring canonical landing.
109	
110	### Step 6 — full pristine gauntlet for canonical pass
111	
112	`tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` on both platforms. This is the canonical gate; the three-fixture iteration is the early-signal cycle.
113	
114	## Lessons captured
115	
116	The three-attempt arc, briefly (full lineage in the companion memo):
117	
118	1. **Attempt 1 — compose rename `transit → auplink` (experiment, reverted).** Worked, exploited Docker's undocumented alphabetical-first network-name heuristic. Rejected as canonical for cross-runtime portability.
119	
120	2. **Attempt 2 — source-CIDR exclusion at PREROUTING + FORWARD.** Stage 1 (with rename) green; stage 2 (no rename) red. Stage 2 failure most likely explained by missing MASQUERADE or missing ESTABLISHED,RELATED rule in RBM-FORWARD (or both); the bridge-gateway-source-rewrite hypothesis that Round 3 used to justify abandoning source-CIDR was empirically wrong on Docker Desktop (actual source is `192.168.65.1`, outside enclave CIDR).
121	
122	3. **Attempt 3 — topology reframe (publisher on pentacle).** Failed empirically. Root cause: bottle's default-via-sentry routing makes inbound published-port traffic produce asymmetric return paths through sentry's transit interface, where Docker Desktop's NAT reflection breaks the symmetry hostilely. Architecturally clean in isolation but fundamentally incompatible with our egress-gate-via-default-route security model.
123	
124	The fix in this memo is **what pre-BBABC's design would have looked like if written defensively against future framework changes**: the same DNAT + MASQUERADE + FORWARD-ACCEPT shape, but with classification by destination port (not interface or source IP) and authorization by conntrack state (not interface or source IP). The original design's principle — sentry takes ownership of its security envelope — is preserved.
125	
126	## Open questions
127	
128	Narrowed considerably after the scry capture. Remaining:
129	
130	1. **Empirically confirm an ESTABLISHED,RELATED rule exists in RBM-FORWARD** during this implementation. Read `rbjs_sentry.sh`; if absent, add it. This is likely the missing piece in attempt 2 stage 2.
131	2. **Docker Desktop reflection-through-transit mechanism** — interesting but not blocking. The fix is independent of this behavior. Defer to a future Round 5 if Podman parity work needs deeper trace of Desktop networking internals.
132	3. **iptables vs nftables inside sentry under Podman** — still open from the companion memo. Run `iptables -V` and `iptables-save` inside sentry under Podman Netavark when Podman validation begins.
133	4. **Future entry-mode use cases requiring original-client IP** — out of scope. The fix preserves the same source-IP-visibility property the original architecture had (bottle sees sentry's enclave IP, not the original client); if a future use case needs original-client IP, add an application-layer proxy. Documented in the companion memo's Round 4 listener-source-IP table.
134	
135	## References
136	
137	- Companion memo: `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md` — full diagnostic history, four rounds of web research, source URLs.
138	- `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh` — sentry startup script; entry-port iptables to be restored here.
139	- `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml` — sentry/pentacle/bottle service definitions; `ports:` directive to be moved back to sentry.
140	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` (around lines 1597-1646) — srjcl test cases including `_jupyter_running` and `_jupyter_connectivity`.
141	- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs` (around lines 1961-2048) — `net-dnat-entry-reflection` ifrit invariant; must remain preserved.
142	- `Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh` and `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh` — scry diagnostic infrastructure to be retained.
143	- Pace ₢A_AAb (sentry-entry-port-dnat-forward-rescope) — this work.
144	- Commit `c8b218ef` (and successors) — the topology reframe to be reverted. Reference for what to undo.
145
